entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Integration with SAP Datasphere for Replication Flow, Data Flow and Model Import
entry.content:
• 1. Installation and Data Provisioning Agent in Windows
  • 2. Connectivity and Configuration of Data Provisioning Agent
  • 2.1 Register Adapters with SAP Datasphere
  • 2.2 JDBC Library Configuration
  • 3. Preparing Data Provisioning Agent Connectivity
  • 4. Preparing SAP Cloud Connector to Connect with SAP Datasphere
  • 5. Creating Datasphere Connection
  • 6. Import Entities from SAP S/4HANA On Premise Via "CSN Exposure: services (BASIS >=756)
  • 7. Model Import Configuration
  • 8. Appendix
  • Conclusion

 

1. Installation and Data Provisioning Agent in Windows

Prerequisite

SAP Datasphere
SAP DP AGENT for Windows

SAP JAVA 8.X

The Data Provisioning Agent, also called HANA DP Agent, has smart data integration Adapters that acts as the communication interface between SAP HANA, ABAP, MS-SQL, DB2 and Oracle. It can be thought like a pipeline to establish data flow, while the connection between the systems and SAP Datasphere is the vehicle that helps transport the data to your SAP Datasphere.

Download the latest version of DP Agent from SAP Support Portal. Once logged in simply do a search for HANA DP AGENT 2.0

Download latest SW

Once it is downloaded copy it to the desired Windows Server.

Once the installation is completed, a windows service will be created and can be accessed from services.msc like below

Under the log On Tab, it may show the default setting for the HANA DP AGENT is set to this account for windows it should be set log on as Local System Account.

Select the Local System account.
Select Allow service to interact with desktop. 
Click Ok to save the change.
Start or restart the service.

 

2. Connectivity and Configuration of Data Provisioning Agent

 

Connect and Configure the Data Provisioning Agent | SAP Help Portal

Need to Logon to the desired Windows Server

agentcli.bat --configAgent

Choose SAP HANA Connection from option 7 and then choose Option 2 Connect to SAP Datawarehouse Cloud via JDBC

“Ensure that the agent is running and try again.”

Check Agent status:

CMD As Administrator

Enter option 1. Start Agent

Check OS Service status: Running

Recheck Agent Status…

 

Not running ?

Solution: Do not use the CMD to start or stop the agent.

Start directly in Win Services.

Check status in CMD

Continue with Configuration:

Check Agent status

Open SAP Datasphere tenant

Check Agent status

Open the Data Sphere URL Screen and Navigate to System -> Configuration.
Then under Data Integration Tab the Agent Details can be found.

2.1 Register Adapters with SAP Datasphere

Register Adapters with SAP Datasphere | SAP Help Portal

All Adapters registered except for the OracleLogReader

2.2 JDBC Library Configuration

SAP HANA smart data integration and all its patches (SAP HANA SDI 2.0) Product Availability Matrix (PAM) First published on: December 14, 2016 Last update: February 13, 2017 Current applicability: SAP HANA smart data integration and all its patches SAP HANA smart data quality and all its patches SAP HANA SDI 2.0 SP00 (with HANA 2.0 SPS00)

Download libs as advised.

JDBC and UCP Downloads page (oracle.com)

3. Preparing Data Provisioning Agent Connectivity

 

Pinging the Data Provisioning Host Serve. Note down the Public IP it’s going out to.
If required get this information from the Network Team Colleague.

Need to add the Public IP in the Allowlist Tab in the Datasphere Application

Request FW rule also from SCC to DWC
 To find DWC IP’s:

Clicking ->  (System) ->  (About) and expanding the More section in the dialog

Firewall Rules

3153915 - IP Addresses Used for Incoming and Outgoing Traffic in Context of SAP HANA Cloud Database - SAP for Me

4. Preparing SAP Cloud Connector to Connect with SAP Datasphere

https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/datasphere/4535969591.html

First need to create a dedicated Sub Account for Datasphere

Below information needs to filled up with the Sub Account details of SAP Datasphere.

Created a Datasphere Subaccount in SAP Cloud Connector

Here RFC connection needs to be created to enable the Replication Flow, Data Flow and to enable the Remote Table in the respective Datasphere Connection that we will configure later

Since there is only one Application Server for DEV environment there is no need to give the details of the message server. Hence only Application server name and it’s virtual name is used to set up the configuration.

One the RFC Connection is created for the Datasphere, need to add the below resources along with their naming policy on the right.

Once complete test the connection and check for any errors.

5. Creating Datasphere Connection

Login to the Datasphere Application page and navigate to the connection option in the left pane.
Then need to select the Desired Workspace the user wants to login.

Click on the + sign to create the connection
In this use case SAP S/4HANA On-Premise Connection type is selected.

Enter the below details as per the landscape information.

For username and password there are recommended roles and authorizations to follow.

Follow the below link for Authorization recommendations.

https://help.sap.com/docs/HANA_SMART_DATA_INTEGRATION/7952ef28a6914997abc01745fef1b607/bcc0ff2acd6a4476b2912ff4cd71cd91.html

Once the configuration is complete the screen below can be seen.
Note: Model Import is yet to be covered further in this document

6. Import Entities from SAP S/4HANA On Premise Via "CSN Exposure: services (BASIS >=756)

Required Search Connectors

This scenario requires certain ESH (Enterprise Search) search connectors to be active and searchable in the SAP S/4HANA On-Premise back-end system. This is the list of required CDS connectors:

To check if they are active on the system

Launch transaction ESH_TEST_SEARCH
Do F4 on field ‘Connector Id’

The list of active searchable connectors is displayed

Do Ctrl+F and search for ‘csn’

Ensure that the 5 required search connectors are found, and that the ‘Searchable’ flag has ‘X’ in all of them

If some of the connectors are missing:
Execute report ESH_CDSABAP_ACTIVATION
Deactivate the option ‘Activate All’ and all other options
In the field Single CDS-Entity, enter the CDS entity corresponding to the connector to be activated

Repeat this procedure for each missing search connector.
Check again in ESH_TEST_SEARCH that the search connectors are now active and searchable

Enter the System Alias
Enter Service Group ID = CSN_EXPOSURE_V4
Click Get Service Groups

CSN_EXPOSURE_V4 will be found. Select it and click Publish Service Groups.

Now the service is published.

Check that the ‘CSN Exposure Service’ is Published

Need to execute transaction /iwfnd/v4_admin
Double click on the System Alias

The list of published services is shown. Verify that the service CSN_EXPOSURE_V4 is in the list

If the Service is not in the list.
Go back to transaction /iwfnd/v4_admin and use option 'Publish Service Groups'

Enter the System Alias and click Get Services

7. Model Import Configuration

Prerequisites

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ANALYTICS_CLOUD/18850a0e13944f53aa8a8b7c094ea29e/c1cbf644b0a1434fbb4ea072a0562286.html?version=2026.2

Execute Transaction Code SICF and enter the Service Path value

/sap/bw/ina

Verify the recommended services are active

Check and verify if the parameter icf/cors_enabled is maintained in the profile Parameter and has the value 1.

Execute the UCONCOCKPIT and from the scenario dropdown field select HTTP Allowlist Scenario

Edit the settings and double click on Cross-Origin Resource Sharing

Edit the below entry by double clicking on it

In the Host Rule section enter the Datasphere Host Name detail.

Add one entry SAP-CONTEXTID in the Allowed Headers field.

A list will appear with the services that can be activated.
Locate ESH_SEARCH_SRV - Version 1
Click on "Add Selected Service" and confirm the proposed default settings.

Navigate to the System option on the Bottom left pane.
Then navigate to Configuration.
Under Data Integration Tab click on Manage Live Data Connection

One the Manage Live Data Screen Opens, click on + sign to create a new connection.

Expand the Data Source Type and then Select SAP S/4HANA on the Left Hand Side

Note: Give the virtual host name and port name in the Host and Port field.
It is recommended as per SAP configuration guide.

Give the credential of the dedicated Datasphere backend user created in S/4HANA ABAP system.

It is important to tick the check box at the end to save the user credentials properly

Once the connection is saved, come back to Datasphere connection screen and edit the connection to enable Model import feature

Here from the drop down select the desired Live Data Connection (Tunnel). For development DEVDSP which was created was selected.

Now Validate the Connection and check if the screen below is coming

8. Appendix

Import Entities from SAP S/4HANA On-Premise via "CSN Exposure" Service (BASIS >= 756)

https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/datasphere/5280816551.html#ImportEntitiesfromSAPS/4HANAOnPremisevia%22CSNExposure%22Service(BASIS%3E=756)-CheckthattheESH_SEARCH_SRVserviceisactive

How to setup mapping (RFC connection) in Cloud Connector for a SAP S/4HANA On-Premise connection

https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/datasphere/4535969777.html

How to setup mapping (HTTPS connection) in Cloud Connector for an SAP S/4HANA On-Premise connection

https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/datasphere/5202583166.html

How to create SAP S/4HANA On-Premise Connection for Data Flow and Replication Flow

https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/datasphere/5227195964.html

Troubleshooting Replication Flow Scenario

https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/datasphere/4428871840.html

How to create SAP S/4HANA On-Premise Connection for Model Import

https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/datasphere/5227195976.html

 

Conclusion

From an architectural perspective, integrating SAP S/4HANA with SAP Datasphere establishes a tightly governed hybrid data landscape that balances real-time access with scalable data persistence.

By strategically combining Remote Tables for virtualized access, Replication Flow for controlled data ingestion, Data Flow for transformation logic, and Model Import for semantic reuse, the architecture supports multiple data consumption patterns without duplicating unnecessary workloads. The connectivity layer—secured through SAP Cloud Connector and appropriate communication arrangements—ensures encrypted, controlled data exchange between on-premise and cloud environments.

This layered design separates concerns effectively:

Source Layer (S/4HANA) – Operational integrity and transactional consistency
Integration Layer – Secure connectivity, replication control, and transformation
Semantic Layer (Datasphere) – Business modeling, harmonization, and data governance
Consumption Layer – Analytics, planning, and reporting tools

Such an architecture not only enables performance optimization and governance alignment but also prepares the landscape for extensibility with advanced analytics, cross-system data federation, and future cloud-native innovations.

Ultimately, the integration is not just a connectivity setup—it is a deliberate architectural evolution toward a clean core strategy and a scalable enterprise data platform.

entry.title: Re: To run production process in sap public cloud what ar...
entry.content:
Good day @kkumara 

Thank you for your question.

In Public Cloud, production supply from an EWM managed warehouse to an IM production storage location is supported as a delivery-based production process. In this design, material staging creates an outbound delivery from production and that delivery is transferred to the warehouse execution flow for picking, printing and confirmation. See Understanding SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, Warehouse Management for more details.

For your expectation, it is important to note that:

  • Order release alone does not guarantee that a picking document is created.
  • The usual trigger is material staging. When staging runs successfully, the system creates the outbound delivery that the warehouse team works with.

Your Stage Material for Production Order Level not fetching the order number could be based on a few reasons such as:

  1. You did not activate the relevant scope item or prerequisites are not configured.
  2. Missing shipping and delivery customising prevents the system from creating staging deliveries, so the staging selection and follow up steps do not behave as the system expects. See KBA 2960232 for more information.
  3. Delivery type determination for the plant and storage location is not available yet, which is typically tied to scope activation in Public Cloud.

Consult these references for more details

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3670761

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3654464

Should you be missing activation scope item 5HO, this can prevent material staging and you may require creating a support ticket for activation. Also note that missing customising that blocks delivery creation from the staging app, including SSCUI 101926 and SSCUI 101303 and in some cases SSCUI 180019.

Consult these references for more details

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3670761

As a next step, I recommend you validate the production order is released and has component demand, run staging in the Stage Materials for Production app,

then verify that an outbound delivery order exists for warehouse execution and printing through Manage Outbound Delivery Orders and output management.

See the following reference for more insights

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/87f9b54f9c4f4e75aff0061860a6589a/93f35227d4574d8bbfebacbb8f2cef22.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/87f9b54f9c4f4e75aff0061860a6589a/a95004e8a7fb4b7e932b06fc42f0ea4c.html?locale=en-US

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Claude Code + Python + SAP GUI Scripting: Your AI Agent for Any SAP Transaction
entry.content:
@Kiran_Kapasi you can ask claude: 

What about Zero Data Retention (ZDR)?

Zero Data Retention is available for Enterprise accounts and must be enabled per organization by your account team. Claude This means prompts and responses are not stored at all after processing.

entry.title: Re: Knowledge Graphs on SAP HANA: From Zero to Enterprise RAG with Triple Store
entry.content:
Hej, thanks for this comprehensive write-up.
You are storing the extracted triples in a table. Have you considered using the "real" HANA native triple store (aka "Knowledge Graph engine")? This might be a overhead for your retrieval strategy since you are looking at one-hop traversals, but it will expose your graph to more complex, SPARQL queries.
Since your focus is HANA Cloud anyhow, you may want to use the native in-database text embeddings (instead of openAI).
And finally, regex string matching might be good enough, but for a more powerful alternative you could look at fuzzy search.

entry.title: Enhancing On-Premise Automation with SAP Automation Pilot and SAP Cloud Connector
entry.content:
Introduction

As organizations continue to operate across increasingly complex hybrid landscapes, the ability to automate tasks securely across cloud and on‑premise environments becomes essential. SAP Automation Pilot, combined with SAP Cloud Connector, delivers exactly that - an integrated approach that allows teams to orchestrate and execute automation scenarios involving systems that are not directly reachable from the public Internet. This powerful pairing enables secure, controlled, and efficient operations, helping teams streamline workflows while maintaining strict network boundaries.

 

 

What is SAP Automation Pilot?

SAP Automation Pilot is a cloud-based service within SAP BTP that streamlines DevOps processes. This low-code/no-code engine offers over 300 pre-built commands for various DevOps tasks, while also allowing users to create custom commands. Highly scalable and integrating seamlessly with SAP services and third-party tools, SAP Automation Pilot empowers teams to reduce operational overhead and enhance efficiency across their technology stack. More details about the product can be found at:

  • SAP Discovery Center

  • SAP Automation Pilot Documentation

 

 

Learn more about SAP Cloud Connector

SAP Cloud Connector provides a secure and efficient bridge between SAP BTP applications and on‑premise systems. With its straightforward setup and clear configuration options, it enables organizations to tap into their existing on‑prem assets without exposing their full internal landscape.

Operating as an on‑premises agent, it uses a reverse‑invoke approach to ensure safe communication with SAP BTP - no inbound traffic required. Built for enterprise continuity, it supports high‑availability scenarios, automatically restores broken connections, and delivers detailed audit logging. The result is a reliable, controlled, and secure foundation for business‑critical integrations. More details can be found at:

  • SAP BTP Connectivity

 

 

Integrating SAP Automation Pilot with SAP Cloud Connector

SAP Automation Pilot and SAP Cloud Connector work together to extend automation into private, on‑premise environments, securely and without opening inbound access. Automation Pilot orchestrates and executes operational tasks, while the Cloud Connector provides the protected bridge to internal systems. SAP Automation Pilot extends several of its standard commands to work seamlessly with systems accessed through the Cloud Connector:

  • Executing HTTP Requests

You can trigger HTTP calls to internal services exposed via the Cloud Connector. The familiar HttpRequest command has been enhanced with additional parameters that enable Cloud Connector usage, making it easy to route requests securely to on‑premise systems. When making HTTP requests through the Cloud Connector, it’s best to increase the default timeout value, as these calls may take longer due to additional network routing and processing. Additionally, when using the HTTPS protocol, make sure to explicitly set the port to 443 in the URL to ensure the request is routed correctly through the connector. For more information, see Executing HTTP Requests.

  • Sending Emails

If your organization relies on private SMTP servers, these can also be accessed through the Cloud Connector. The SendEmail command now supports additional input keys, allowing Automation Pilot to send emails through internal mail servers without exposing them externally. For more information, see Sending Emails.

  • Using Jenkins Commands

All Jenkins commands in the Automation Pilot catalog now support Cloud Connector integration as well. This is especially useful if you operate your own private Jenkins instance, enabling secure automation scenarios that interact directly with your internal CI/CD infrastructure. For more information, see Using Jenkins Commands.

 

 

 

Executing an HTTP GET Request Through the Cloud Connector

Prerequisites

1.     You have a Cloud Connector installation. See Installation.

2.     You have connected the Cloud Connector to the subaccount where SAP Automation Pilot is enabled. See Adding and Managing Subaccounts.

3.     You have specified the internal system that you want to access in the Cloud Connector. This will allow SAP Automation Pilot to access the system. The procedure is specific to the protocol that you are using for communication. For more information, see Configure Access Control.

4.     You have ensured that the internal system can make outbound calls to the region-specific Automation Pilot Connectivity Proxy endpoint. This connection is often blocked by default due to corporate outbound firewall rules, so you might need to add the endpoints to the firewall's allowlist. See a list of the Automation Pilot Connectivity Proxy endpoints in Service Availability.

These steps are also linked in SAP Automation Pilot Documentation.

  • Once the Cloud Connector is successfully linked, you can view all relevant connection details directly in the Cloud Connectors view in the SAP BTP cockpit that can be found uder Connectivity. This includes the Location ID, connected Resources, Host, Protocol, Back-End type, and Description. For the next steps, you will need the Location ID.

 

 

  • In our example setup, we’ve created a command in Automation Pilot that includes two executors configured to reach an on‑premise HTTP endpoint. For the first executor, choose Edit to configure the required fields. In Method, from the dropdown menu, choose GET, then provide the URL of the endpoint you want to access.

 

 

  • In the Cloud Connector tab, located in the Smart View of the command, turn on the toggle to enable the Cloud Connector and enter the corresponding Location ID, ensuring the request is sent through the correct on‑premise connection.

 

 

  • For the second executor, we follow the same configuration approach as before, this time supplying a different URL so the command can target another on‑premise endpoint exposed through the Cloud Connector.

 

 

  • If you are using OAuth authentication, you can also switch on the Technical User Propagation toggle. This allows SAP Automation Pilot to forward the identity of the technical user defined through the OAuth client. In practice, this means the request can access the on‑premise system on behalf of that technical user, ensuring the correct authorizations are applied without requiring additional credentials. For more information, see SAP BTP Connectivity.

 

 

 

 

Customer Success Story: Johnson Controls International (JCI)

Thanks to the integration of SAP Automation Pilot with SAP Cloud Connector, JCI was able to operate its large and complex on‑premise SAP landscape securely. JCI eliminated over 3,000 hours of manual work and transformed routine monitoring into a strategic capability.

You can read the full customer story here 👉  SAP Community Blog: “SAP Automation Pilot – Empowering IT Operations with AI Insights”

 

 

Wrap-Up

The integration between SAP Automation Pilot and SAP Cloud Connector provides a secure and efficient way to automate operations across hybrid landscapes. With extended command support, reliable on‑premise access, and seamless execution of internal requests, teams can simplify their workflows and improve operational consistency. This combination delivers the flexibility and control needed to automate confidently in complex enterprise environments.

entry.title: Re: Build, Configure, and Deploy SAP CAP Projects to the Cloud
entry.content:
Hi @Deep7432,

This issue usually occurs when a custom UI5 application does not route API calls through the Managed Approuter after deployment.

When running locally, the UI5 app directly calls the CAP service (eg : /catalog/Books).

After deployment to Cloud Foundry, requests must go through the HTML5 Managed Approuter and Destination.

  1. Why the List Report Works

    The Fiori Elements List Report wizard automatically configures:

    Destination to the CAP service,Correct datasource configuration,Proper routing through the Managed Approuter

  2. Why the Custom UI5 App Fails

    In custom UI5 apps, if the service URL is defined directly for eg : /catalog/Books it may bypass the destination.This causes 403 Forbidden errors because authentication tokens are not forwarded.

  3. Correct Configuration in manifest.json

    Define the service in the datasources section
"dataSources": {
"mainService": {
"uri": "/catalog/",
"type": "OData",
"settings": {
"odataVersion": "4.0"
}
}
}

entry.title: Re: SAP Build Work Zone: Tile Opens(content federation) but App Fails with ‘IP Address Cannot Be Fou
entry.content:
Hi @OmPrakashJha 

The issue seems to be in the Long URL which is generated by the work zone. Try to change the below as per KBA 3392044, it should work:

Run Time Destination: Change the Run Time Destination from  s4fioricontentrt to s4

Change the Sub Account Name from exexis-sap-build-work-zone-dev to exexis-dev

 

Rest things we dont have control to change. I hope it wil fix your issue.


Regards,

Rohit Gera
